pages.text: SCLC specimens, compressive strength and splitting tensile strength were deﬁned according to TS EN 12390-3 and TS EN 12390-6 standards, respectively, by 2000 kN compressive machine with a rate of loading controller. The compressive and splitting tensile strength of SCC and SCLCs were tested on cube specimens (150 /C2 150 /C2 150 mm) aged 3, 7, 28 and 90 days. Strain dates were taken on the 28 day aged cylindrical specimens under axial compression by a transducer appara- tus. The linear variable displacement transducers used was in sensitivity of 0.002 mm for obtaining strain data. The average of three specimens was used for deﬁning the stress–strain curve. Each strain value was obtained for every 25 kN. Fi- nally, the strain versus stress ﬁgure was drawn for each series of SCC and SCLC. SCC and SCLC specimens with 70 /C2 70 /C2 70 mm dimensions were subjected to abrasion test at 28 days after casting. Before testing, specimens were dried in an oven at 55 ± 5 /C176C until reaching a constant weight. Dorry test apparatus was used for the abrasion test ( Fig. 1 ) according to ASTM C779. In the abrasion system, 0.3 kN load was applied on the specimens. In the test procedure, 20 g of corun- dum-crystalline powder was spread on the disc, the specimens were then placed, the load was applied to the specimen and the disc was rotated. Totally 440 periods were applied for each specimen. After that, the length loss due to wear was measured. 2.4. Physical tests A test to determine the sorptivity of specimens was also carried out at the 28th day. For each mixture, three cubic specimens, at the dimensions of 70 /C2 70 /C2 70 mm, were prepared. The specimens were kept in standard water curing till the 28 days. The capillarity coefﬁcients were determined by using specimens precondi- tioned and dried in an oven at about 105 ± 5 /C176C to the constant mass. As shown in Fig. 2 , the test specimen was exposed to water through a plane cross-section (70 /C2 70 mm) by placing it in a pan. The water level in the pan was maintained at about 5 mm above the bottom of the specimen during this experiment. The sur- faces of the specimen below the water level were coated with parafﬁn to achieve unidirectional ﬂow. At regular times such as 1st, 4th, 9th, 16th, 25th and 36th min, the mass of the specimen was measured using a balance, then the amount of water adsorbed was calculated and normalized regarding the cross-section area of the specimen [13,14]. In horizontal axis, square root of time; and in vertical axis, water absorption quantities in unit volume of specimens ( q) were used. To determine the capillarity coefﬁcient, q was plotted against the square root of time ( pt), then, the sorptivity (k) was calculated from the slope of the linear relation between q and pt. Therefore, capillarity coefﬁcient was obtained for each SCC and SCLC series by Eq. (2.1),a s follows: q ¼ k /C1 pt ð2:1Þ where k is capillarity coefﬁcient (cm 2/min); q is water absorption quantity in cross- section of specimens (cm 3/cm2); and t is time (min). The thermal conductivity of specimens was determined by the ‘‘hot wire meth- od’’ according to ASTM C1113 on the aged 28 days specimens. Unit weight, speciﬁc porosity and water absorption were calculated for 28 days aged specimens accord- ing to Archimedes principle by the weight measurements of saturated specimens in air and in water, and dry weight (oven drying at 105 /C176C to constant weight). Besides, microstructure (interfacial transition

The interaction between the cement paste matrix and the light- weight aggregate is different from that with normal concrete [15,5]. Even tough there is an ITZ in SCC; it was not observed in SCLC with all the type of LWA because of roughly surface texture of aggregates. Pumice has half-open porous surface texture while tuff has roughly surfaced. These roughly surface textures increased the interconnection of cement matrix and lightweight aggregate (Fig. 4 b and c). It can be noted that interlocking is better than nor- mal aggregate in interfacial zone of hydrated cement matrix and LWA because of the surface of LWA is more roughly than normal aggregate surface. It may be concluded that the wavy surface of LWA increases the holding of hydrated cement matrix or mortar on the aggregate. When ITZ of SCLC with diatomite was investigated (Fig. 4d), it would be seen that no interlocking was observed. The weakness bond can be clearly seen. This weakness can be due to the high por- ous structure of diatomite aggregate. Moisture exchange takes place between the partially saturated lightweight aggregate and plastic mortar phase. Reducing the tendency of fresh SCLC develops thin ﬁlms of water at the interface between the aggregate and ce- ment paste. This phenomenon is also known as ‘‘wall effect” in the literature. Because of thin water ﬁlm between aggregate and paste, formation of mechanical interlocking is prevented [5,16]. 3.3. Compressive strength The development of compressive strength of SCC containing the crushed limestone aggregate is given in Fig. 5 , showing the strengths at 3, 7, 28 and 90 days. It was observed that compressive strength of SCC increased by improving the age of concrete. The compressive strength for all specimens increased with time regardless of water to binder ratio. The range of strength varied from 27 to 35 MPa at 3 days, from 38 to 47 MPa at 7 days, from 41 to 52 MPa at 28 days and from 45 to 51 at 90 days, depending on water to binder ratio. It can be also clearly seen that there was about 68% and 90% enhancement in compressive strength of SCC by increasing of concrete age from 3 to 28 and from 7 to 28 days, respectively, for w–b ratio of 0.36. Increase in compressive strength of SCC by age mainly depends on strength gaining of con- crete with time. This enhancement was 64% and 86% with concrete age from 3 to 28 and from 7 to 28, respectively, in 0.48 w–b ratio. Increase of w–b ratio resulted with decrease of strength develop- ment because of decreasing the hydrated products with increase of porous structure in concrete. Strength development is higher in low w–b ratio than in high w–b ratio due to more homogeny dis- tribution of cement particles with high content chemical admix- ture in cement paste. In Figs. 6–8, compressive strength of SCLC with pumice, tuff and diatomite LWA was presented, respectively. The compressive Fig. 4. Microstructure of: (a) SCC; and SCLC with (b) pumice; (c) tuff; (d) diatomite (1: aggregate; 2: hydrated cement matrix; 3: air bubble due to air entraining admixture). 0 10 20 30 40 50 60 70 0 1 02 03 04 05 06 07 08 09 0 Curing age, days Compressive strength, MPa 0.36 0.40 0.43 0.46 0.48 Fig. 5. Compressive strength of SCC versus curing time. _I.Bekir Topçu, T. Uygunog˘ lu / Construction and Building Materials 24 (2010) 1286–1295 1289

strength decreases as the water to binder ratio for each series is in- creased. Moreover, a replace of crushed stone with lightweight aggregate resulted in decrease of compressive strength. LWA has a very signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the strength properties of light- weight concrete. The inﬂuence of strength properties of cement paste increases with increasing density of aggregate. In addition to strength properties of lightweight concrete, all other properties of LWAC are also controlled by properties of the aggregate. The reduction in compressive strength is related to the weakness of lightweight aggregates. When the compressive strength of SCLC with pumice varied between 15 and 27 MPa, the compressive strength varied between 16 and 25 MPa and between 10 and 22 MPa for the SCLC series with tuff and diatomite, respectively, in water–binder ratio of 0.36 depending on the concrete age. Although pumice aggregate has lower weak strength than tuff, the highest compressive strength values were obtained in SCLC with pumice due to high interlocking with cement paste and half-open porous surface of pumice. On the other hand, the lowest compressive strength was obtained in SCLC with diatomite be- cause of both high weakness of diatomite and weak bond strength in ITZ of hydrated cement matrix and aggregate. By the way, the range of strength development of SCLC with pumice, tuff and diatomite, respectively, varied from 61% to 52%, from 72% to 56% and from 51% to 44%, depending on water to bin- der ratio, by increasing of concrete age from 3 to 28. It varied be- tween 95% and 67%; between 82% and 80%; and between 92% and 75% for the SCLC series with pumice, tuff and diatomite, respectively, by increasing of concrete age from 7 to 28, depending on water–binder ratio. Furthermore, SCLC series with pumice and tuff are in class of structural LWAC because of compressive strength of specimens at 28th days is higher than 17 MPa [7]. But SCLC with diatomite is in class of structural LWAC just for 0.36 w–b ratio. 3.4. Splitting tensile strength When effect of aggregates type on splitting strength of SCC and SCLC was considered, it can be noted that the splitting strength of concretes showed a considerable increase depending on decrease of w–b from 0.48 to 0.36 and concrete age from 3 to 90 days (Figs. 9–12). Though the SCC and SCLC possess the lowest strength in early ages, they have a satisfactory strength enhancement rate with increase of curing time. Splitting strength of SCC versus curing time. 0.0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.5 4.0 4.5 0 1 02 03 04 05 06 07 08 09 0 Curing age, days Splitting strength, MPa 0.36 0.40 0.43 0.46 0.48 Fig. 10. Splitting strength of SCLC with pumice versus curing time. 1290 _I.Bekir Topçu, T. Uyg

from 2.43 to 3.90 MPa at 28 days; from 2.69 to 4.23 MPa at 90 days depending on w–b ratio. In splitting test, failure of SCC specimens occurs mainly at the weak interfacial zone between cement paste and normal aggregate because of strength of normal aggregate greater than strength of bond. When the splitting strength of SCLC with pumice, tuff and diatomite is between 1.74–1.35 MPa, 1.23– 1.49 MPa and 0.82–1.14 MPa, respectively, at 28 days; it ranged from 1.42 to 2.02 MPa, from 1.31 to 1.64 and from 1.0 to 1.30 MPa, respectively, at 90 days, depending on water to binder ratio. As seen, the lowest splitting strength was obtained in SCLC with diatomite. This is because the failure of these specimens oc- curred mainly at the weak interfacial zone between cement paste and diatomite. But failure of SCLC with pumice and tuff occurred in aggregate because of strong bong strength between cement ma- trix and roughly surface aggregate. On the other hand, strength development in SCLC with pumice (Fig. 10 ) showed better results compared to SCLC with tuff. Correlation between the splitting tensile strength and compres- sive strength of SCC mixtures with natural and LWA is presented in Fig. 13, regardless of water to binder ratio, aggregate type and con- crete age. Suitable linear relation is developed between splitting tensile strength and compressive strength for various grades of SCC ranges from 5 to 61 MPa. It is clearly seen from the Fig. 13 that splitting tensile strength linearly increases with the increase of cube compressive strength of mixtures. A relation has been pro- posed, which is suitable for all the type of SCC and SCLC. The pro- posed equation is given by: ft ¼ 0:0602 /C1 fc þ 0:2009 ð3:1Þ where ft is splitting tensile strength (MPa); and fc is cube compres- sive strength (MPa). 3.5. Stress–strain relation and modulus of elasticity The strain capability under stress and modulus of elasticity of concrete are fundamental parameters necessary in structural analysis for the determination of the displacements and strain dis- tributions, especially when the design is based on elasticity consid- erations. Effect of aggregate type on strain ability of SCC and SCLC under the compressive force is given in Fig. 14. Strain of SCC series (Fig. 14 a) corresponding to the maximum stress of the stress– strain curve was largest at the lowest water–binder ratio. However, the strain ability of SCCs decreased rapidly with the increase of w– b ratio. The stress–strain relationship of concrete has a curved shape, due to the fact that aggregate and cement paste, having dif- ferent stiffness characteristics, are connected in one bearing sys- tem. In concrete types where the stiffness of the matrix is close to the stiffness of the aggregate, the stress–strain relationship of concrete will also be close to linear. Stress–strain curves of SCC and SCLC with all the type of LWA are typically linear to level of approximately 90% of the failure strength, especially, in low water–binder ratios. However, SCC has higher strain than that of plain or high strength concrete corresponding to the similar stress. This is due to that SCC contains lower coarse aggregate than plain or high strength concrete. In the SCLC with pumice, tuff and diatomite (Fig. 14 b–d), low strength and very high strain corresponding to the maximum stress were found when compared to stress–strain curves of SCC. With increasing water to binder ratio, however, strength decreased rapidly and the s

that for a given concrete type, a lower w–b leads to a higher mod- ulus of elasticity. However, replacement of LWA with natural crushed stone causes a decrease in modulus of elasticity compared with SCC due to lower strength of LWAs. The modulus of elasticity of SCC ranged between 39 and 23.2 GPa depending on w–b ratio. While the modulus of elasticity of SCLC samples with pumice aggregate changed between 18 and 5 GPa, the modulus of elasticity of SCLC of those made up of tuff and diatomite aggregates ranged between 11–6.5 GPa and 9.4–3.9 GPa for each w–b ratio, respec- tively. When comparing the SCC, decrease in the modulus of elas- ticity, due to the replacement of pumice with natural aggregate, was between 53% and 78% for w–b ratio of 0.36–0.48. However, the decrease in SCLC with tuff and diatomite was between 71.8% and 72%; and between 76% and 83.2%, respectively. In SCLC mix- tures, the highest and the lowest modulus of elasticity values were obtained in SCLC with pumice and with diatomite, respectively, for water to binder ratio due to the strain ability of these mixtures un- der the compression stress. For both SCC and SCLC, the relationship between the compres- sive strength and the modulus of elasticity is presented in Fig. 16 . A good relation was found between the compressive strength and modulus of elasticity for both concrete types. Correla- tion coefﬁcient is 0.90 and 0.89 for SCC and SCLC, respectively. As a function of compressive strength, modulus of elasticity of SCC and SCLC can be determined by: ESCC ¼ 1:6334 /C1 fc /C0 47:59 ð3:2Þ ESCLC ¼ 1:14 /C1 fc /C0 11:34 ð3:3Þ where E is elastic moduli (GPa); and fc is characteristic cube com- pressive strength (MPa). 3.6. Abrasion strength The abrasion resistance of concrete is strongly inﬂuenced by the compressive strength, surface ﬁnishing techniques, curing types, aggregate properties and testing conditions, i.e. dry or wet. The abrasion depths of SCC and SCLCs are shown in Fig. 17. The test re- sults show that the abrasion depth of the SCC, produced in water to binder ratio of 0.36, 0.40, 0.43, 0.46 and 0.48, was 12.5, 13.5, 14, 15 and 15.5 mm, respectively, after 440 rotations of disc. Comparison of modulus of elasticity of SCC and SCLC. 1292 _I.Bekir Topçu, T. Uygunog˘ lu / Construction and Building Materials 24 (2010) 1286–1295

with pumice, tuff and diatomite, the abrasion depth ranged be- tween 29.5 and 40 mm; between 29 and 43.5 mm; and between 28 and 44.5 mm, respectively. This means that the abrasion resis- tance of SCC is better than the SCLC with all the type of LWA regardless of water to binder ratio. This is based upon the fact that lightweight aggregates are not as strong as crushed stone. Further- more, it must be noted that crushed limestone aggregate has high- er wear resistance than all type of LWAs (see Table 2). Loss of wear resistance in pumice, tuff and diatomite aggregate was 56%, 52% and 64% when compared to that of crushed limestone aggregate. When water to binder ratio was considered, the abrasion depth of SCC and SCLC increased with an increase in w–b ratio from 0.36 to 0.48 due to increase of porous structure in hydrated cement matrix. When comparing the SCC, increase in the abrasion depth of SCLC of those made up of pumice, tuff and diatomite was 57%, 56% and 55%, respectively, for the water to binder ratio of 0.36; and it was 61%, 64% and 65%, respectively, for the 0.48 w–b ratio. In the SCLC, the highest abrasion depth was found in SCLC with diatomite due to high wear loss and weak bond strength between aggregate and cement matrix. Even tough pumice aggregate has lower wear resistance than tuff aggregate, SCLC with pumice has lower abra- sion depth by increase of w–b ratio. This was because of the surface of the pumice has half-open texture, which leads to the chemical reaction between the cement and water. This made the bond still stronger even at high w–b ratios. One of the important ﬁndings of this study was relationship be- tween abrasion depth and compressive strength for SCC and SCLC. There are two views on the relationship between compressive strength and abrasion resistance of concrete. One of them is that the abrasion resistance of concrete increases with increasing com- pressive strength [18–20], while the other view is that the abrasion resistance of concrete is independent on compressive strength [21,22]. The relationship between abrasion resistance and com- pressive strength of concrete is obtained from this test, and shown in Fig. 18. It can be seen from Fig. 18 that the abrasion depth of SCC and SCLC, regardless of w–b ratio and aggregate type, decreased with increasing compressive strength. In other words, the abrasion resistance of SCC and SCLC increased with an increase of the com- pressive strength. The correlation coefﬁcient is very close to 1.0. The abrasion depth ( L A) of SCC and SCLC versus compressive strength at 28th day ( fc) can be deﬁned by Eq. (3.4) as following LA ¼ 60:47 e/C0 0:0311/C1 fc ðLA; mmÞ; ðfc ; MPaÞð 3:4Þ 3.7. Unit weight and porosity The unit weights of SCC and SCLC series were measured at right after 28-day curing. The density of structural SCLC varies from 1342 to 1717 kg/m 3 compared with 2157–2211 kg/m 3 for SCC. It can be seen from Table 5 that the unit weight of concretes de- creases with an increase in w–b ratios. While the unit weights of SCC samples that in 0.36–48 w–b ratios changed between 2211 and 2157 kg/m 3, the unit weight of SCLCs of those made up of pumice, tuff and diatomite ranged between 1535 and 1394 kg/ y = 1.6334x - 47.587 R2 = 0.8088 (SCC)y = 1.1386x - 11.34 R2 = 0.7973 (SCLC) 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 50 Compressive strength, fc (MPa) Modulus of elasticity, E (GPa) Fig. 16. Relationship between modulus of elasticity and compressive strength i

4. Conclusions An extensive study was performed on physical and mechanical properties of self-consolidating lightweight aggregate concrete, and results were compared with the properties of SCC. Obtained results were summarized below: /C15 Compressive strength and splitting tensile strength of concretes showed a considerable increase depending on decrease of w–b from 0.48 to 0.36 and concrete age from 3 to 90 days. Suitable linear relation is developed between tensile strength and com- pressive strength for various grades of SCC and SCLC ranges from 5 to 61 MPa. /C15 In the SCLC with pumice, tuff and diatomite, lower strength and higher strain capability corresponding to the maximum stress were found when compared to stress–strain curves of SCC. Moreover, replacement of LWA with natural crushed stone causes a decrease in modulus of elasticity compared with SCC due to lower strength of LWAs. However, suitable relations are found between modulus of elasticity and compressive strength of different grades of SCC and SCLC. /C15 Abrasion resistance of SCC is better than the SCLC with all the type of LWA regardless of water to binder ratio. The abrasion resistance of SCC and SCLC increases with increasing compres- sive strength. It was also found that the abrasion resistance of concrete is strongly dependent on their compressive strength. /C15 It was found that the unit weight of SCC was decreased in ratio of 30–35%, 22–31% and 34–38% by replacing of the crushed limestone with pumice, tuff and diatomite, respectively. This means that if SCLC was used, dead load of structures would be decreased in ratio of 22–38%. /C15 Replacement of crushed limestone aggregate with natural LWA in SCC causes an increase in absorption because of high water absorption ability of lightweight aggregates compared with crushed limestone. /C15 The sorptivity of SCC was similar with that of SCLC containing the pumice and tuff aggregates for all the w–b ratios of mix- tures. This was probably due to that the pumice and tuff aggre- gates different from diatomite aggregate as surface texture and water absorption value, and sorptivity is highly related with capillar spaces in mortar of concrete. Pumice and tuff aggregates were strongly surrounded by hydrated cement matrix, and sorp- tivity of aggregates prevented by strong bond. /C15 SCC and SCLC has higher thermal conductivity coefﬁcient than plain and structural lightweight concrete. This difference due to that powder material and paste content was used in high ratio in the SCC and SCLC to achieve the good workability. Moreover, replacing pumice, tuff and diatomite instead of crushed lime- stone increased the insulation of the SCC mixture average in ratio of 58%, 35% and 60%, respectively. This means that if SCLC was used in the constructions, indirectly, energy consumption in the structures would be decreased in ratio of 35–60%. Consequently, when lightweight aggregate type was considered in the SCLC, pumice is fairly useful aggregate for production of SCLC with high interlocking in ITZ, high compressive and splitting tensile strength, low unit weight and high insulation properties. References [1] Yang CC, Huang R. A two-phase model for predicting the compressive strength of concrete. Cem Concr Res 1996;26(10):1567–77. [2] Su N, Hsu KC, Chai HW. A simple mix design method for self-compacting concrete. Cem Concr Res 2001;31(12):1799–807. [3] Persson B. A comparison between mechanical properties of self-compacting concrete

Effect of aggregate type on properties of hardened self-consolidating lightweight concrete (SCLC) _Ilker Bekir Topçu a, Tayfun Uygunog˘ lu b,* a Eskis/C223ehir Osmangazi University, Civil Engineering Department, 26480 Eskis /C223ehir, Turkey b Afyon Kocatepe University, Technical Education Faculty, Construction Department, 03200 Afyonkarahisar, Turkey article info Article history: Received 14 January 2009 Received in revised form 1 December 2009 Accepted 3 December 2009 Available online 4 January 2010 Keywords: Self-consolidating concrete Lightweight aggregate type Physical properties Mechanical properties abstract In this study, the effects of aggregate type on the physical and mechanical properties of hardened self- consolidating concrete produced with lightweight aggregate (SCLC) were investigated. In experiments, three coarse lightweight aggregate (LWA) types, pumice, volcanic tuff and diatomite, and normal lime- stone aggregate were used. Different combinations of water to binder ratio and superplasticizer dosage levels were prepared for the SCLC mixtures. The total powder content (cement and mineral additives) was constant in the experiments. Physical properties such as thermal conductivity, dry unit weight, porosity and capillarity; mechanical properties such as compressive and splitting tensile strength, mod- ulus of elasticity and abrasion resistance were determined in hardened condition. The results, in general, showed that SCLC with LWA in lower unit weight has lower mechanical and physical properties except for thermal properties when compared to properties of SCC. /C2112009 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Self-consolidating concrete (SCC) is a composite material with coarse and ﬁne aggregates embedded in a cement paste matrix having high ﬂow-ability. As in any composite material, the physical and chemical properties of the constituents and the interaction be- tween them determine the behaviour of the material [1]. The mechanical behaviour and the durability of the SCC are affected by the aggregate used. Aggregates generally constitute about 60% by volume of SCC. Due to the large volume fraction it occupies in SCC, aggregates exert a major inﬂuence on the characteristic prop- erties of SCC, and can be expected to have an important inﬂuence on other properties as well [2]. SCC contains powder material as constituent in addition to the traditional concrete materials such as sand, coarse aggregate, admixture and water. Therefore, in gen- eral, the density of SCC is much higher when compared with the density of plain concrete [3,4]. Use of lightweight aggregates in the production of SCC is generally easy way for reduce the unit weight of SCC. Thus, reducing the mass of the structure or building that is related with unit weight of SCC is of utmost importance to reduce their risk due to earthquake acceleration [5]. In practice, lightweight aggregates often have lower particle densities than the density of the mortar matrix and natural aggre- gates in concrete. The coarse aggregates move the upward with vibration of plain concrete, and thus segregation may take place during the transporting and placing the fresh concrete [6]. Self- consolidating of concrete is one of the unique properties for pre- venting of movement of lightweight aggregates (LWA) upward due to vibration [7]. In the literature there are few studies on the use of lightweight aggregate (LWA) in the production of SCC [8–12]. However, most of them a
